artifacts.title: Which receipt from 2025-02-01
artifacts.excerpt:
i've been off of Twitter for quite a while, having went on a climbing trip with a group of friends. 

although i've been back for a few days, I've been hesitant to return to this platform in full.

even though it may seem like it, social media is not a reflection of reality, but rather fantasy. when you escape, just for a moment, you realize this. 

the majority of the content you see are thoughts pregnant with deep emotion and fantasy, and through this digital space, we spiral into an almost dream-like state, although it is even less real than a dream. 

as a whole, social media seems to me to be a sort of collective, mass fantasy with hints of de-realization, and i can't help but realize that the way most of us use this platform are guiltily of participating, espousing our own emotionally charged rhetoric divorced from life experience, but married to unconscious emotion.

when you are

artifacts.title: Which receipt from 2026-02-01
artifacts.excerpt:
Carl Jung is correct in saying that “everything has a shadow”. Within Christianity, this is known as “the world”. Yet he is incorrect in saying that evil is ‘a priori’, that is, innate, for despite his genius, he was unable to see that it was the Fall that cast the first shadow.

Having fallen from grace with Eden, humanity fell into the valley, living in the shadow of darkness, and what was good was obscured.

The gnostics, drunk on their own depravity, clouded their pre-lapsarian memory. They could see that the light sat atop the mountain, but they could not see that that mountain was their origin, that their “process of individuation”, that is, their search for gnosis, was not an attempt at a first ascent of the mountain, which they conquer through their will and inner light, but rather a coming home, the return of the prodigal son, who is guided home by a light outside of himself.

H

artifacts.title: Jung receipt from 2025-02-04
artifacts.excerpt:
How can ‘the Self’ contain evil if the psyche is a self-regulating system that aspires towards wholeness?

According to Carl Jung:

"The psyche is a self-regulating system that maintains its equilibrium just as the body does. Every process that goes too far immediately and inevitably calls forth compensations, and without these there would be neither a normal metabolism nor a normal psyche. 

In this sense we can take the theory of compensation as a basic law of psychic behaviour. Too little on one side results in too much on the other. Similarly, the relation between conscious and unconscious is compensatory."

Extending the metaphor of the body in regard to the Self, when the body is undamaged, it is both whole and good. Certainly, a healthy body capable of running, jumping, laughing, and procreating is categorically "good" and a desirable state. However, if I were to get cut by a knif
artifacts.proof_of_search: Community Archive row → normalized post → topic planet. Terms: jung, evil, other, body.

artifacts.title: Life receipt from 2025-08-30
artifacts.excerpt: an artist cannot proceed with life if he has not put forth into the world what ricochets in his heart. he will be stunted, for the new life of a finished product is precisely what frees up space for new life in his heart.
artifacts.proof_of_search: Community Archive row → normalized post → topic planet. Terms: life, only, man, live.

artifacts.title: Life receipt from 2025-01-08
artifacts.excerpt:
i have been quite disenchanted with the illusory nature of Twitter as of recently.

when it's at its best, Twitter undeniably provides incredible value.

at its worst, Twitter distracts, sucks, steals, and quite honestly makes us ill.

in other words, Twitter is like walking through the red light district to reach the library or a Bible study.

if i do not take periodic brakes, I find myself saying things I do not mean, thinking thoughts that are not my own, and believing things that are quite unreal.

but it is imperative to have a presence here, since so much of life has been digitally outsourced.

just make sure that as you build your audience, you do not become as flat and two-dimensional as the screen you type on.
artifacts.proof_of_search: Community Archive row → normalized post → topic planet. Terms: life, only, man, live.

artifacts.title: Life receipt from 2025-02-07
artifacts.excerpt:
be open to any and all friendships. 

until proven otherwise, you have no enemies.

there are many great teachers out there whose lessons can only be learned through friendship.

friendships come in varying degrees, and each degree is endearing and special in their own ways.

of course, each friend doesn’t necessarily need to access to the entirety of you.

you could have a beautiful friendship with someone you only see at the café. a quick chat with a familiar face can markedly change the tempo of your day, and there is a tremendous depth in that.

your capacity to connect with others reflects your capacity to differentiate your love with others.

this, in part, is a key aspect of re-enchanting your life. 

there are times for solitude, certainly, but no man is an island.
artifacts.proof_of_search: Community Archive row → normalized post → topic planet. Terms: life, only, man, live.

planets.name: Only
planets.summary: Archive cluster around only: 85 posts; 4 quote-backed receipts pinned.

artifacts.title: Only receipt from 2025-11-09
artifacts.excerpt:
i am convinced that screen use suppresses memory. 

whenever i abstain and find myself on a long walk, memories from long ago appear out of thin air, important moments that contain valuable information and emotions that i have forgotten, but must remember in order to move forward.

ofc they were always there, always seeking to be recognized and understood, but they had been continually suppressed by the malefic hypnagogic state that screen use induces.

it creates artificial worlds to take us away from our own, and by design. consequentially, we forget who we are and where we came from. we accept visions, ideas, and identities from entities we know nothing of. we lose our memory, lose our way, and lose our individuality. whether we scroll or post, we do not escape this fact.

yet when we look away and center ourselves on reality, we wake up from the lucid dream that is social media. memo
